comments.title: Death
comments.content:
You've written before about overcoming fear of death relatively easily just by pondering it. In my experience, there's a very big difference between a conceptual understanding of something and an intuitive/embodied knowing of something. It seems like fear of death would only be removed fully by the experience of death. So people telling me that some part of me lives on, or deciding that reincarnation is real doesn't really help. Do you really live with the gnosis that death will not affect you? Is contemplating/imagining my self dying enough? It seems like this is a fundamental motivation/fear that is not so easily overcome.

-Isaac Hill

replies.title: Re: Death
replies.content: I don't recall ever saying that just pondering death is enough to stop being afraid of it. In my case, I worked in nursing homes for four and a half years, and had my share of corpses to wash up and get ready for the undertaker, some of them patients who I'd been fairly close to. I also have quite a few memories, not only of previous lives, but of several previous deaths, including the last one, which was pretty messy. (I was in the "death seat" of a car that was in a head-on collision at fairly high speed.) So I've had the experience of death, both others' and my own, and for that reason it doesn' bother me greatly.

replies.title: Re: Death
replies.content: Ouch. Do you have memories of the period between lives as well?

replies.title: Re: Death
replies.content: They're very murky and unclear, but a few scraps, yes.

replies.title: Re: Death
replies.content: If I may ask, what kinds of recollections are they?

replies.title: Re: Death
replies.content: Mostly dim memories of emotional states. For a variety of reasons, I was really upset about the way that life ended.

replies.title: Re: Death
replies.content: Why would many people be so afraid of dying if they already had this experience often? Am I correct to assume that is because many people don't remember previous lifes, and thus, don't remember previous deaths?

replies.title: Re: Death
replies.content: Exactly. If you don't remember, you don't have that source of insight.

replies.title: Re: Death
replies.content:
Ok, thank you for your response, that makes more sense. I may have been mistaken that you said it was merely pondering death, but I don't have the time to look through all the old posts and check (I am a long time reader and very infrequent commenter, part of which is because though I find magic fascinating, my goals are really more of a mystics.) I am asking this because I have started contemplating myself dying as the Buddha recommends and it's very hard to really feel it... imagining a coyote ripping out my throat after falling down a gully and breaking my leg is visceral, but only about as much as watching a gory movie. And imagining my body being cremated is emotionally cathartic, but not exceptionally so. It seems that there are deeper layers that this sort of meditation only scratches at. Perhaps it would be good to volunteer at a hospice or something like that.

Best,
Isaac Hill

replies.title: Re: Death
replies.content: Remember that the Buddha lived in a place and time when it wasn't at all uncommon to see a dead person -- people didn't hide from death then the way Americans do now. Thus I'd encourage you to volunteer at a hospice or the like -- yes, that'll give you a better sense of what death is, and of course it's also a compassionate thing to do, and thus highly recommended by most of the mystics I know of.

replies.title: Re: Death
replies.content:
Death and the Buddha--according to the founding legend, it was having been sheltered from the facts of sickness and death as a pampered prince, followed by running away from his palace and confronting an aged person, an ill person and a corpse, that shook Siddhartha out of complacency and int the spiritual search that ultimately led to his teachings. In our modern life we are like that papered prince in being sheltered from many aspects of age, illness and death. The aged are in care homes, the terminally ill are in hospitals and the dead are either out of sight or in funerals with viewings they are all prettied up.

Rita

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Sure. In a daily shield chart reading, the right witness is you, the left witness is what the day has in store for you, and the judge is the outcome. he first triplicity expands on what's going on in your inner life that day; the second, what events will influence you; the third, what's going on in your environment; and the fourth, what's affecting your friends and associates. In each triplicity, the upper figure on the right is the past, the upper figure on the left is the future, and the lower figure (the niece) is the present. When the same figure appears in any two or more of these positions, it represents something that bridges these categories -- for example, if you've got Fortuna Major in the second and fourth triplicities, something good is going to happen to you and to one or more people you know. Other details? Let the daily charts themselves teach you.

The triplicities are much less important than the judge and witnesses, so it's best to read them as minor adjustments to the main message. In the first reading, it would amount to something like "It's a good idea, but there are a few drawbacks, and these include some minor losses in terms of friends and associates."

I notice that the fourth triplicity in the second reading is nearly as bad as in the first -- you've got two bad figures and one good figure. So either way you have problems with friends, associates, and authority figures!

I'm twenty years older than our host, so I was already in my thirties when this shift started to affect the whole nation.

I think it's mostly been a change of direction as a very old pendulum in American cultural history swings back and forth from one extreme to another: now toward permissive tendencies, now toward repressive ones. Each swing seems to me to take roughly 30 years. There's no over-all progress as the pendulum swings, but there is some change in the direction of the swing each time around. (Think of a Foucault's Pendulum, changing the apparent direction of its unchanging swing rather slowly, because the earth rotates beneath it as it swings.)

In part, however, it looked to me very much as though the shift was deliberately engineered by a number of specific people who used Ronald Reagan as their convenient "meat-puppet" of a front man, first in California, then on the national stage. They are now very old men at very conservative think-tanks (like the Heritage Foundation), and in another decade or two they will all be gone. They got lucky: their political and cultural program happened to mesh with the swing of the pendulum--of which pendulum, IMHO, they remained quite unaware.

replies.content:
My take on the matter of "what happened in the Eighties":

1. Social movements are tied to Neptune, which entered Capricorn in 1984. Capricorn's been touted as a possible fall for Neptune (the other is Uranus-ruled Aquarius). I'm inclined to agree with this perception, as it makes more intuitive sense for Neptune to be exalted in lunar Cancer than in the more self-oriented Leo. We'd expect something of a slam-on-the-brakes to active social movements when Neptune entered its fall.

2. Meanwhile, Pluto, still a planet at that time, entered Scorpio (its rulership) from Libra in 1983, heightening its influence and giving the entire period 1983-95 a Plutonian vibe. As has been suggested in previous discussions, Pluto (not Neptune) rules oil or at least the oil industry, so it makes sense that oil made a comeback at the expense of its rivals.

3. The Baby Boomers, especially the draft-fighting vanguard of that generation, started having their Saturn returns in the late seventies and early eighties - they'd lost some of the idealism of youth and were saying things like "holy shale, changing the world is HARD". From a Fourth Turning standpoint, it's the process of going from an Awakening to an Unraveling - not exactly a pleasant process if you were flourishing during the Awakening.

4. To an extent, the Sixties/Seventies had gone too far, too fast, and in a multitude of things. Such a sudden and sweeping revolution tends to result in a counter-revolution... and sometimes the counter-revolution wins. This may seem like a heresy to the religion of Progress and thus unallowable - but with the economy improving, the pontiffs of Progress could easily jump ship from the perceived moral/cultural progress of the Awakening to the perceived economic progress of the Reagan years.

Arguably, the same sort of thing should be happening right now (the stock market isn't really a barometer of economic health, but it's perceived as one and it is at record highs), but it hasn't. I suspect this is in part because:
- (a) The sociocultural fragmentation of the 80s and 90s that was enabled (in the addiction sense) by the ascendancy of the Internet hadn't happened yet back then, and
- (b) The pontiffs of Progress had invested so much mental and psychocultural capital in the persona of Hillary as what essentially amounted to a religious icon that they cannot adapt in as fluid a manner as they did during the 80s. Jimmy Carter had never been viewed as Progress personified - let alone exalted as it.

- Barrigan

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The crucial thing about transits, and it's something that a lot of people miss, is that a transit just activates a portion of your natal chart -- it doesn't add anything new. A planet that affects your natal Mars by transit, say, just gives your Mars a nudge, getting it to express a little more of its positive or negative side, as the case may be.

comments.title: Bronze age
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid and the forum,
I could never quite figure how was it that ancient civilization preferred to use bronze – a metal mix requiring long commercial routes and complex knowledge to produce – instead of the readily available iron, much simpler to extract and transform. They could also use iron, mostly for work tools, so they knew how to extract it, but for weapons the mostly used bronze. If I understand correctly, iron is quite aggressive for spiritual entities. Could it be that the then warriors didn’t use iron to avoid hurting their friends in the spiritual plane? Did anyone see this hypothesis advanced somewhere? Or is it just nonsense?
Elodie

replies.title: Re: Bronze age
replies.content:
Hi Elodie,

Most metallurgists would say the explanation is straightforward: bronze is made from copper and tin, both of which are easy to extract from ore and can be easily melted together in a pot over a fire. Iron requires much higher temperatures to extract it from ore and smelt it, so specialized furnaces and much more fuel were required. Working iron just wasn't worth it until the trade routes collapsed and there was no other choice.

Ms. Krieger

replies.title: Re: Bronze age
replies.content: The version I've heard is not so much that the trade routes collapsed as that the preferred sources of copper were becoming harder and harder to mine, at which point working iron became more economical by comparison.

replies.title: Re: Bronze age
replies.content:
Actually, I've heard exactly the opposite from historians. Copper working preceded bronze working; copper ores are common and require relatively low temperatures. Bronze working followed as a natural progression.

Iron working requires higher temperatures and a completely different skill set on the part of the smiths. It didn't take off until after the collapse of Bronze Age civilization when the source of tin from present-day Afghanistan was shut off.

Bronze Age bronzes vary widely in hardness depending on the proportion of copper and tin. Some alloys are harder than anything that iron can produce until the invention of steel and later modern alloys. In other words, properly produced bronze weapons and armor were superior to anything that could be produced in iron at the time.

John Roth

replies.title: Re: Bronze age
replies.content:
My understanding is that it's even simpler than that. Copper's easy to smelt from ore -- the general theory for years has been that somebody built a fire in a circle of rocks, one of the rocks was copper ore, and some of the copper melted out right then and there -- and so is tin. Old methods of making bronze were really simple: put a lot of copper ore and a little tin ore in the smelting pot, heat 'em up, and pour the molten liquid that results into the mold.

Iron is *not* easy to smelt from ore -- it takes much higher temperatures, and it's fussy in ways that copper is not. It also has to be forged, not merely cast, to be able to hold an edge. Through most of the Bronze Age, in fact, iron was rare and precious -- one of Tutankhamen's pieces of jewelry is an eye of Horus with the pupil made of a little piece of meteoric iron, the most expensive metal in the world in his time.

What changed the equation is that people in Asia Minor somehow figured out the trick of smelting iron from ore and forging it into tools and weapons. That caused immense changes -- bronze was pricey enough that it was a monopoly of the rich, while there were vast amounts of iron ore in those days and so it could be made very cheaply. So all of a sudden everyone had sharp spears, knives, and the like. You're right that this would have caused serious shifts in human relationships with certain spiritual beings, but that doesn't seem to have been a causal factor.

replies.title: Re: Tiphareth v Solar consciousness
replies.content:
Actually, the Adeptus Minor ritual is a good touchstone for the difference. It's supposed to foster Tiphareth consciousness, but that only happens when the candidate really is ready to part the Veil. Otherwise, you get solar consciousness, and very often unbalanced solar consciousness at that. The difference? Solar consciousness is ego-centered. If you've done the necessary work and equilibrated the various forces of your personality, solar consciousness can give you a healthy, well-adjusted ego in command of the other aspects of the self; if you're not well balanced, you get the kind of flaming egomaniac so embarrassingly common in the GD scene.

Tiphareth consciousness is not ego-centered. What happens in that case is that the lower self comes into contact with the Higher Self, and the ego takes on a secondary role in the structure of the personality.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG.
Life has tossed me a huge curve ball (several really, but I discuss just one now), and it seems so uncanny as to suggest spiritual influences that have me gasping:

April 23 was my younger daughter’s (Lindsay) 9th birthday, and I went to meet my family, including in-laws, at a restaurant that evening. I arrived first, and read a book (Taleb’s Fooled by Randomness) while I waited at the reserved table. My family arrives, and my older daughter Jenny, who is 15, seems disturbed. After they got seated, my wife explains that Jenny was really freaked out when she entered the restaurant looking for me, and thought she saw me sitting across the room. Jenny approached the table to sit beside “me”, then saw that the man she approached was not me, but had a such a startling physical resemblance that he could have been my twin, and she literally ran out without noticing me in plain sight across the room; I was the only other person there. I had been so engrossed by my book that I didn’t notice her. My wife points to the man, still seated, whom she initially mistook for me as well. I see him, and it is truly an uncanny experience to see someone who could be your own double: same profile, facial features and proportions, same skin color, a tad older looking and weather-beaten perhaps, same haircut with same shade of grey, same height and build. He was wearing solid black. At several points during our meal, the man and I make brief eye contact, and there is a real gravitas about him. I intended to make some small-talk with him when he leaves, since he’ll need to walk past our table. But soon he is gone, and neither my wife nor I saw him leave. It was just like that scene from your own novel, Weird of Hali, Innsmouth.

Back at our home, we have a little family gathering for Lindsay’s birthday. My cell phone rings; it shows an out-of-town number, and I feel uneasy so I ignore it. Then our land line rings, which my wife answers, and it’s the neighbor of my 91 year-old mother, who informs us that she just called the paramedics for my mom who was incapacitated by weakness. My mom is hospitalized, and after several days, the doctors complete an initial workup, finding heart failure largely due to a severely narrowed valve, a severe anemia from both iron deficiency and an intestinal bleed. There are extensive enlarged lymph nodes deep in her chest, lung masses (both findings very suspicious for cancer), and other lung disease impairing her breathing. The bleeding, lymph nodes and masses cannot be evaluated because invasive procedures are required, and mom is too fragile to undergo such. So she is a hospice patient, and I drive nearly a thousand miles to get her and bring her home, and she is now in my home with home hospice care.

My mom and I never had a close relationship; I have long thought she was autistic as she has extremely sparse, unrevealing speech, she never had friends or socialized at all as I was growing up, and always had a fixed “reptilian stare” or mask-like face; even old photos of her with me as a toddler show this, and I think either I did not bond with her, or the bond was broken when I was 3 or 4, as evidenced by a nightmare I had then in which I dreamed she was decapitated, then re-appeared as a department store manikin. I awoke from that nightmare with grief and terror that even now remains one of my clearest, and frequently recalled, memories. I am 60 now. (And yes, this has had a big negative effect on my life and relationships, and yes I have been-there-done-that with multiple counselors and psychotherapists over the years…). I have for decades been undertaking what my wife somewhat derisively calls “bibliotherapy”, as I have made all my progress with introspection, studying, literature, meditation, writing, prayer and lately some esoteric work. One of the books I’ve recently been reading, I’m too frazzled now to recall which, was emphasizing the central importance of getting in touch with your living parents, as that is foundational in dealing with any serious issues that have been dogging you from childhood. I recall thinking that sort of advice is not realistic for me, and now here she is in my house, and we have had the first normal conversation I can recall; it wasn’t the heavens parting or anything like that, it was just having a normal conversation such as you might feel when you are connected with a family member.

I don’t expect to have mom around for more than a few weeks. I need to use this time to see what I can do to unblock whatever it is in my past that has been dogging me.

Now there is something else that may not be significant, but it’s on my mind: I’ve been looking to join the the Freemasons, and the process is underway. I recently submitted my formal application, and I was just contacted a few days ago regarding a visit and interview with my family. I had to postpone that as I was bringing my mother home for hospice. My wife is suspicious of Freemasonry, and is not too happy with my interest; I’ve been trying to persuade her speak to a close friend of hers who happens to be the daughter of a Freemason (my sponsor in fact), but she seems in no hurry.

Here is why I am writing: That double of me in the restaurant has me spooked, to speak nothing of the prescient synchronicity with my mom’s final illness. I fear that something was being communicated to me, but I failed to get the message. I considered doing a geomatic divination, but I don’t have the sense that my skills are up to the task; as I generally present it with questions that are pretty closed-ended, and I don’t think I have the chops to ask/frame a question properly. What do you recommend?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That does indeed sound spooky! Don't worry about whether or not you got the message, though. If the universe needs you to know something, it'll get to you. Your double may have been any number of things, but I suspect it was a crisis apparition -- a very common kind of phantasm, something weird and usually not actually physically present that shows up when a family member is facing a life-or-death crisis of some kind. As for what I'd recommend, if you're comfortable praying, that would be a very good thing to do just now; calling on positive spiritual influences will help your mother with her dying process and also make things easier on you.

comments.title: Outgrowing Tarot Deck
comments.content:
I spent the last four years slowly making my way through Learning Ritual Magic, finally finishing in January of this year. When I chose a deck of Tarot cards to work with, I chose the Thoth deck. Although it was a slog sometimes working with the deck, it helped me a lot both in learning the Tarot and in learning material such as the Hebrew letters and other correspondences. After some deliberation about what to study next, I have decided to work through Circles of Power. I am currently working on the material for Chapter 2 while keeping up the practices of divination, LRBP/Middle Pillar, and meditation until I get to the parts of the book where I will do other work.

In the meantime, the Thoth deck has seemed to go bad on me. The readings seemed to hammer on the same points over and over in almost a shrill way, without the benefit of actually helping with anything that was happening to me. I've decided it is no longer useful, and have retired it and am currently using the Druid Craft tarot I bought several years back but never used. Already the readings seem more helpful and actually help me understand and face things I need to know right now.

I have two questions concerning the two tarot decks.

1)What is a respectful way to retire the Thoth deck, thank it for its service, and get rid of it? It is like it is calling to me from its place on the shelf and mocking me for not being able to take its readings. I can only describe it as the deck seeming to turn on me and I need to part ways with it.

2)Although the new deck is helping, I can't help but notice it is very different in symbolism from the material in Circles of Power. I am wondering if I should look into another deck like the Rider-Waite deck that works better with the symbolism. Does it matter whether my deck uses the same symbolism or not? I know having symbolism in the deck work with the symbolism in the book really helped me in Learning Ritual Magic.

Thank you for your time--it is great to get perspective on these questions outside the running commentary in my head!

replies.title: Re: Outgrowing Tarot Deck
replies.content:
1) Thank it and burn it. It should not be given to another person, as it will be charged with your own magical energy.

2) You should be able to use the DruidCraft Tarot with Circles -- I did the work that went into it using the Aquarian Deck, which also has different symbolism.

replies.title: Re: Outgrowing Tarot Deck
replies.content:
This is a bit late; I hope you will still see it!

I’ve only worked with the Thoth deck twice, and it is a *bully.* I very much felt as though I had to backhand it with my own will before it would answer my query. Not my cup of tea, and I have subsequently been able to find decks with much better personalities. :) You may need to try a couple until you find ones that work with you, and of course the more you work with the tarot the more insights you will be able to weave together.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Happy Monday, JMG-

Would it be useful to construct a GD equivalent of the Sphere of Protection ritual? Has anyone attempted this?

If yes to the former and no to the latter, what would be the appropriate GD-system equivalent to the telluric current? I suppose it must be distinct from the Earth energy among the four elements, and unlike the sun and moon, earth doesn't appear as a planet in GD's astrology. Is there one?

-A

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There already is one. There was a Golden Dawn offshoot among the sister orders of AODA; one of these days, when I have some spare time (hah!), I should revive it, as I have all the papers and received the initiations.

The solar and telluric currents are specific to Druidry, and were not invoked in the Holy Order of the GD. Rather, divine names and archangels are assigned to each station of the SOP, and so are planets, as follows:

East: YHVH, Raphael, Sun
South: ALHIM, Michael, Mars
West: AL, Gabriel, Mercury
North: ADNI, Uriel, Venus
Below: AGLA, Zadkiel, Saturn
Above: AHIH, Iophiel, Jupiter
Within: YHShVH, one's own guardian angel, Moon
